title: Kawasaki Disease
objectives: Apply diagnostic criteria for classic and incomplete Kawasaki disease
Understand the critical importance of timely treatment
Apply IVIG and aspirin treatment protocols

sections.title: Overview and Epidemiology
sections.content:
Kawasaki disease (KD) is an acute, self-limited vasculitis of unknown etiology that primarily affects medium-sized arteries, especially the coronary arteries.

**Epidemiology:**
• Peak age: 6 months to 5 years (80% of cases)
• Rare in <3 months and >5 years
• Male > Female (1.5:1)
• Higher incidence in Asian populations (Japan highest)
• Leading cause of acquired heart disease in children in developed countries
• Year-round, slight winter/spring peak

**Importance:**
• Untreated: 25% develop coronary artery aneurysms
• Treated (IVIG within 10 days): <5% develop aneurysms
• Early recognition and treatment is CRITICAL

sections.title: Classic Diagnostic Criteria
sections.content:
**Fever ≥5 days PLUS ≥4 of 5 clinical features:**

**1. Bilateral Bulbar Conjunctival Injection**
• Non-exudative (no pus/discharge)
• Limbic sparing (clear zone around iris)

**2. Oral Mucous Membrane Changes**
• Erythema and cracking of lips
• Strawberry tongue (prominent papillae)
• Diffuse oropharyngeal erythema

**3. Cervical Lymphadenopathy**
• Usually unilateral
• ≥1.5 cm diameter
• Often single, firm, non-tender

**4. Polymorphous Rash**
• Maculopapular, morbilliform, or erythema multiforme-like
• NO vesicles or bullae
• Often accentuated in groin

**5. Extremity Changes**
• Acute: Erythema and edema of hands/feet
• Subacute (weeks 2-3): Periungual desquamation (peeling)

**Mnemonic: CRASH and Burn**
Conjunctivitis, Rash, Adenopathy, Strawberry tongue, Hand/foot changes, fever (Burn)

sections.title: Incomplete Kawasaki Disease
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Fever ≥5 days with only 2-3 clinical criteria, but KD suspected

**This is NOT 'atypical' – it's just incomplete presentation**

**Who Gets Incomplete KD:**
• Infants <6 months (highest risk for coronary complications)
• Older children >5 years
• Up to 20% of all KD cases

**When to Suspect:**
• Prolonged unexplained fever in young child
• Fever with some but not all classic features
• Fever with laboratory or echo findings suggestive of KD

**Laboratory Support for Incomplete KD:**
• CRP ≥3.0 mg/dL or ESR ≥40 mm/hr
• Plus ≥3 of:
  - Albumin <3.0 g/dL
  - Anemia for age
  - Elevated ALT
  - Platelets >450,000 after day 7
  - WBC ≥15,000
  - Urine WBC ≥10/hpf (sterile pyuria)

**KEY: Get an echocardiogram if incomplete KD suspected**

sections.title: Laboratory and Cardiac Evaluation
sections.content:
**Typical Laboratory Findings:**

**Inflammatory Markers:**
• CRP and ESR markedly elevated
• WBC often elevated (left shift)
• Platelets: Often normal initially, then thrombocytosis in week 2-3

**Other:**
• Mild normocytic anemia
• Elevated AST/ALT (transaminases)
• Hypoalbuminemia
• Sterile pyuria (urethritis)
• CSF pleocytosis (aseptic meningitis)

**Echocardiography:**
Obtain at diagnosis, 2 weeks, and 6-8 weeks

**Findings to Look For:**
• Coronary artery dilation or aneurysm
• Decreased LV function
• Mitral regurgitation
• Pericardial effusion

**Coronary Aneurysm Classification:**
• Small: <5 mm
• Medium: 5-8 mm
• Giant: >8 mm (highest risk for thrombosis and stenosis)

sections.title: Treatment: IVIG
sections.content:
**IVIG is the cornerstone of treatment**

**Dose:** 2 g/kg IV as a single infusion over 10-12 hours

**Timing:** Within 10 days of fever onset (ideally days 5-10)
• Can still give after day 10 if ongoing inflammation

**Mechanism:**
• Immunomodulatory (exact mechanism unknown)
• Reduces duration of fever
• Dramatically reduces coronary aneurysm risk (25% → <5%)

**Response:**
• Most patients defervesce within 36 hours
• If fever persists >36 hours after IVIG → Retreatment

**IVIG-Resistant KD:**
• ~10-20% of patients
• Options: Second dose IVIG, corticosteroids, infliximab
• Higher risk for coronary complications

**Precautions:**
• Risk of aseptic meningitis post-IVIG
• Delay live vaccines 11 months after IVIG

sections.title: Treatment: Aspirin
sections.content:
**Aspirin is used in ALL KD patients**

**Acute Phase (High-Dose):**
• 80-100 mg/kg/day divided q6h
• Continue until afebrile for 48-72 hours
• Anti-inflammatory effect

**Convalescent Phase (Low-Dose):**
• 3-5 mg/kg/day (antiplatelet dose)
• Continue for 6-8 weeks if no coronary abnormalities
• Continue indefinitely if coronary abnormalities present

**Note on Reye's Syndrome:**
• Risk exists but is very low
• Benefits of aspirin outweigh risks in KD
• If influenza or varicella exposure during aspirin therapy → Consider risk/benefit

**Clopidogrel:**
• May be added for patients with large aneurysms
• Alternative to aspirin if allergic

sections.title: Long-Term Complications and Follow-up
sections.content:
**Coronary Artery Outcomes:**

**If No Coronary Abnormalities:**
• Excellent prognosis
• Low-dose aspirin for 6-8 weeks, then stop
• Single follow-up echo at 6-8 weeks

**If Transient Coronary Dilation:**
• Usually resolves within 4-8 weeks
• Low-dose aspirin until normalization
• Serial echos

**If Persistent Aneurysms:**
• Lifelong cardiology follow-up
• Long-term antiplatelet (±anticoagulation)
• Activity restrictions may apply
• Risk of stenosis, thrombosis, MI

**Giant Aneurysms (>8mm):**
• Highest risk for thrombosis and rupture
• Often require anticoagulation (warfarin)
• May need coronary interventions
• Lifelong cardiac monitoring

sections.title: Differential Diagnosis
sections.content:
**Conditions That Mimic KD:**

**Viral Infections:**
• Measles, adenovirus, EBV, enterovirus
• Scarlet fever (Group A Strep)

**Drug Reactions:**
• Stevens-Johnson syndrome
• Drug hypersensitivity

**Other Vasculitides:**
• Polyarteritis nodosa
• Systemic JIA

**Toxic Shock Syndrome:**
• More toxic appearance
• Hypotension
• Desquamation is late finding

**MIS-C (Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome in Children):**
• Post-COVID inflammatory syndrome
• Overlaps with KD features
• Often older children, more GI symptoms, more cardiac dysfunction
• May have positive COVID serology

**KEY: KD is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion – treat if suspicion is high**

keyTakeaways: Classic KD: Fever ≥5 days + 4/5 criteria (conjunctivitis, oral changes, rash, lymphadenopathy, extremity changes)
Incomplete KD: Fever + 2-3 criteria – get echo and labs, treat if supportive
IVIG 2 g/kg within 10 days reduces aneurysm risk from 25% to <5%
Aspirin: High-dose (anti-inflammatory) during acute phase, low-dose (antiplatelet) for 6-8 weeks
Infants <6 months and incomplete KD have highest risk for coronary complications

references.citation: McCrindle BW, Rowley AH, Newburger JW, et al. Diagnosis, Treatment, and Long-Term Management of Kawasaki Disease: A Scientific Statement for Health Professionals From the American Heart Association. Circulation. 2017;135(17):e927-e999
references.keyContent: Definitive AHA guideline for KD diagnosis, IVIG treatment, and coronary management

references.citation: Jone PN, Tremoulet A, Choueiter N, et al. Update on Diagnosis and Management of Kawasaki Disease: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association. Circulation. 2024;150(23):e481-e500
references.keyContent: 2024 update with high-risk criteria and imaging advances

references.citation: Kobayashi T, Saji T, Otani T, et al. Efficacy of immunoglobulin plus prednisolone for prevention of coronary artery abnormalities in severe Kawasaki disease (RAISE study). Lancet. 2012;379(9826):1613-1620
references.keyContent: RAISE trial - adjunctive steroids for high-risk patients

references.citation: Son MBF, Newburger JW. Kawasaki Disease. Pediatr Rev. 2018;39(2):78-90

reviewQuestions.question: A 3-year-old has had 6 days of fever, bilateral non-exudative conjunctivitis, cracked red lips, a diffuse maculopapular rash, and swollen hands. No lymphadenopathy is noted. What is the diagnosis?
reviewQuestions.explanation: This child has fever ≥5 days plus 4 criteria (conjunctivitis, oral changes, rash, extremity changes). Even without lymphadenopathy, this meets criteria for classic KD. Treat with IVIG and aspirin.

reviewQuestions.question: IVIG is given on day 7 of illness to a child with Kawasaki disease. 48 hours later, the child still has fever of 39°C. What is the next step?
options: Continue observation – IVIG can take time
reviewQuestions.explanation: Persistent fever >36 hours after IVIG indicates IVIG-resistant KD. Treatment options include a second IVIG dose, corticosteroids, or infliximab. These patients have higher coronary risk.

reviewQuestions.question: What is the most important reason for early treatment in Kawasaki disease?
options: To reduce coronary artery aneurysm risk
reviewQuestions.explanation: The primary goal of treatment is preventing coronary artery aneurysms. Without treatment, 25% develop aneurysms; with IVIG within 10 days, <5% do. Early recognition and treatment is critical.
